perturbation.original_text: 1.4 A Party shall promptly notify the other Party of, w ithout limitation, any unauthorized access, use, co pying or disclosure of Confidential Information of which a Party becomes aware and shall use its best endeav ors to terminate such access, use etc. and shall provid e all reasonable assistance requested by the other Party in this connection.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 A Party shall notify the other Party of any unauthorized access, use, copying or disclosure of Confidential Information within 60 days of discovery and will provide assistance at its own discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to 'within 60 days' and removing the obligation to provide 'all reasonable assistance' creates a contradiction, as data breach notification laws often require faster notification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various state data breach notification laws (example: California)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires notification 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay'. A 60-day delay could be considered unreasonable in many data breach scenarios.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 7. Accounting of Disclosures [1798.25 - 1798.29] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798.29. (a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification timeframe from 'promptly' to 'within 60 days'. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both indicate this contradicts California law, which requires notification 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CIV 1798.29(a)) states that an agency must disclose any breach of security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach. Allowing up to 60 days introduces a significant delay that could be deemed unreasonable and thus, non-compliant with the legal requirement for prompt notification. The change removes the 'all reasonable assistance' obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 3.  Remedies 3.1 Each Party shall be liable in accordance with Danis h law for damages (including claims, expenses and indirect losses of whatsoever nature) arising out o f or in connection with that Party’s breach of its obligations under this Agreement, without prejudice to other rights and remedies available including, but not limited to, restrictive injunction and other eq uitable relief.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.  Remedies 3.1 Each Party shall only be liable for direct damages up to a maximum of 10,000 DKK arising out of or in connection with that Party’s breach of its obligations under this Agreement, and no other rights and remedies shall be available.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting liability to only direct damages and capping it at 10,000 DKK ($1450 USD) contradicts Danish law, which generally allows for the recovery of indirect losses unless specifically excluded and does not typically impose such a low cap on liability for breaches of contract.
perturbation.law_citation: Lov om Aftaler og Andre Retshandler på Formuerettens Område (Aftaleloven)
perturbation.law_explanation: Danish contract law generally permits claims for both direct and indirect losses resulting from a breach of contract. The modified clause, by limiting liability to only direct damages up to 10,000 DKK, attempts to circumvent this general principle and may be deemed unreasonable or unenforceable under Danish law, especially if the actual losses are far greater and reasonably foreseeable.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for liability for damages under Danish law, without limitation. The changed contract limits liability to direct damages up to 10,000 DKK and states that no other remedies shall be available. The Perturbation Explanation states that this contradicts Danish law, which generally allows for recovery of indirect losses unless specifically excluded and does not typically impose such a low cap on liability. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that Danish contract law permits claims for both direct and indirect losses and that limiting liability to direct damages up to 10,000 DKK may be deemed unreasonable or unenforceable. The absence of scraped law snippets does not change the determination of the contradiction as the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both clearly state how Danish law is contradicted by the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Law and venue 4.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed i n accordance with Danish law. 4 4.2 Any dispute arising between the Parties under or as a result of this Agreement shall be settled by Dan ish Institute of Arbitration in accordance with the rul es of the Danish Institute of Arbitration in force at the time when such proceedings are commenced. However, restrictive injunctions and similar actions may be sought from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Law and venue 4.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, USA. 4.2 Any dispute arising between the Parties under or as a result of this Agreement shall be settled by binding arbitration in a closed-door process with no right to appeal and no discovery allowed.
perturbation.explanation: Selecting Delaware law and specifying 'no discovery allowed' may contradict Delaware's rules of civil procedure regarding discovery in arbitration. While parties can limit discovery, a complete prohibition could be challenged if it prevents a fair hearing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware allows for arbitration agreements, prohibiting *any* discovery whatsoever could be seen as hindering the arbitrator's ability to fairly resolve the dispute, potentially conflicting with the principles of fair arbitration under Delaware law. Section 5709 grants arbitrators the authority to permit discovery.

Uniform Arbitration Act Â§ 5701. Effect of arbitration agreement. A written agreement to submit to arbitration any controversy existing at or arising after the effective date of the agreement is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract, without regard to the justiciable character of the controversy, and confers jurisdiction on the Chancery Court of the State to enforce it and to enter judgment on an award. In determining any matter arising under this chapter, the Court shall not consider whether the claim with respect to which arbitration is sought is tenable, or otherwise pass upon the merits of the dispute. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause specifying 'no discovery allowed' in arbitration, which potentially contradicts Delaware law. While parties can limit discovery, a complete prohibition might hinder a fair hearing, conflicting with principles of fair arbitration. Section 5709 grants arbitrators the authority to permit discovery. The scraped snippet 1 from Title 10, Chapter 57, Â§ 5701 states that written agreements to submit to arbitration are valid, enforceable, and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. If prohibiting any discovery makes the arbitration unfair, it could be grounds to challenge the agreement. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction by potentially undermining the fairness of the arbitration process as envisioned under Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Effect This Agreement shall become effective upon signing, but shall apply also to Confidential Information disclosed to or received by a Party prior to signin g. , on _______________ , on _______________ For Central Denmark Region For ____________________________________ ______________ ____________________ [Name]
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Effect This Agreement shall become effective retroactively to January 1, 1900, regardless of when signing occurs. , on _______________ , on _______________ For Central Denmark Region For ____________________________________ ______________ ____________________ [Name]
perturbation.explanation: Making the agreement effective retroactively to January 1, 1900, is likely unenforceable under Danish law (and most legal systems) due to issues of impossibility, lack of consideration for that period, and potentially violating laws in effect at that time. It also creates a risk that statutes of limitations would have already expired.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Danish Law on Limitation of Actions (Forældelsesloven)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Danish Law on Limitation of Actions sets specific periods for claims. Setting the effective date to 1900 means any claim arising from actions related to confidential information before the modern statute of limitations took effect would now be time-barred, contradicting the intended scope and application of that law and common-sense principles of valid agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Retsinformation You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract text, making the agreement retroactively effective to January 1, 1900, creates a significant legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that this retroactive date would mean any claims arising before the modern statute of limitations took effect would now be time-barred. This contradicts the intended scope and application of the law and common-sense principles of valid agreement. The Perturbation Explanation states that it also creates a risk that statutes of limitations would have already expired. This falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the time-sensitive obligations are altered in a manner that violates legal requirements and makes the contractual terms unenforceable.
